title: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
author: James Silberman
orientation: A January 2021 Free the States report on the filing of HB1539, the Indiana Protection at Conception Act, by Reps. Curt Nisly and John Jacob, detailing the bill's text, its committee assignment, and the political context surrounding its fifth consecutive introduction. The article argues that abolition has growing momentum in Indiana following Jacob's primary upset and the rise of Abolish Abortion Indiana.
audiences: Christian pro-lifer weighing abolition vs. regulation
Constitutional law student or federalism observer

questions.question: What does HB1539 actually do, and how soon would it take effect?
questions.answer: HB1539, the Indiana Protection at Conception Act, would totally and immediately end abortion in Indiana by adding a new Article 51 to the state code that asserts a compelling state interest in protecting human life from the moment of fertilization.
questions.quote: HB1539 would totally and immediately abolish abortion in the state. It has been assigned to the Indiana House Judiciary Committee, which is made up of nine Republicans and four Democrats, and is chaired by Rep. Jerry Torr (R-Carmel).

questions.question: How does the bill define when human life begins?
questions.audience: Constitutional law student or federalism observer
questions.answer: The bill anchors the protection of life at fertilization, treating the union of human ovum and human sperm as the legal beginning of human physical life subject to Indiana's protection.
questions.quote: Chapter 1 of the new article establishes that life begins “when a human ovum is fertilized by a human sperm.”

questions.question: On what constitutional grounds does the bill claim Indiana can act despite Roe v. Wade?
questions.audience: Constitutional law student or federalism observer
questions.answer: The bill grounds Indiana's authority in the Tenth and Ninth Amendments, the Declaration of Independence, and the consent-of-the-governed origin of both the federal and Indiana governments, asserting that protecting life is a reserved and inalienable-rights matter.
questions.quote:
Sec. 2. Indiana’s authority to assert its interest in protecting human physical life from the moment that human physical life begins is drawn from the following:
>
> 1. The Tenth Amendment to the Constitution of the United States, which provides that all powers not delegated to the United States elsewhere in the Constitution are reserved to the states or to the people.
> 2. The Ninth Amendment to the Constitution of the United States, which provides that the enumeration of certain rights within the Constitution must not be construed to deny or disparage other rights retained by the people.
> 3. The Declaration of Independence, which acknowledges that life is endowed to all persons as an inalienable right.
> 4. The fact that the governments of the United States and Indiana were instituted by the consent of the people in 1787 and 1816, respectively, to secure the inalienable rights acknowledged by the Declaration of Independence.

questions.question: What does the bill say Indiana officials should do if a federal court tries to strike it down?
questions.answer: The bill instructs Indiana to treat any contrary federal court decision as nonauthoritative and void, and directs all state officials and agencies to enforce the law regardless of any court ruling against it.
questions.quote: Sec. 4. Indiana’s interest in protecting innocent human physical life from the moment of conception shall be enforced by Indiana government officials and agencies, regardless of any court decision to the contrary.

questions.question: Does the bill criminalize federal agents who try to interfere?
questions.answer: Yes. Section 2 of the enforcement chapter makes it unlawful for any federal official, agent, or contractor to enforce any federal action that interferes with Indiana's protection of life from conception.
questions.quote: Sec. 2. It is unlawful for any official, agent, or employee of the United States government or an employee of a private entity providing services to the United States government to enforce any act, law, treaty, order, rule, or regulation of the United States government that interferes with Indiana’s interest in protecting human physical life from the moment that human physical life begins…

questions.question: How is John Jacob persuading hesitant Christian Republican colleagues?
questions.audience: Christian pro-lifer weighing abolition vs. regulation
questions.answer: Jacob says he confronts fellow Christian legislators directly with a moral ultimatum: regulating abortion is sin, and a Christian has no option but to abolish it.
questions.quote: “I’m having conversations with these guys and I’m saying, ‘If you’re a Christian, you don’t really have an option here. It’s not an option to sit here and say that we’re going to regulate abortion. That is sin. We have to abolish abortion.’”

questions.question: Why is the 2021 session being framed as more hopeful than past attempts?
questions.answer: There is a new Speaker, Todd Huston, who reportedly told Nisly and Jacob he wants to start fresh, and the bill has been moved out of the Public Policy Committee (where it usually died) into the Judiciary Committee.
questions.quote: “The other thing that is different is the that the bill has been assigned to a different committee. In previous years the bill was assigned to The Public Policy Committee, which is where bills go to die. This year it has been assigned to the Judiciary Committee chaired by Jerry Torr.”

questions.question: Who was the previous main obstacle to abolition in Indiana, and how did he treat Nisly?
questions.answer: Former Speaker Brian Bosma, a self-described pro-life Republican, was the chief opponent who publicly called abolition unconstitutional and used his power to sideline Rep. Nisly for supporting it.
questions.quote: Former Speaker Brian Bosma, a pro-life Republican, had been the primary opposition to abolition, once telling reporters that abolishing abortion was unconstitutional. Bosma also used his power to sideline Nisly because of Nisly’s support of abolishing abortion.

questions.question: What does Jacob say his primary win means for the rest of the Indiana GOP?
questions.answer: Jacob argues his upset of incumbent Republican Dollyne Sherman shows voters are done tolerating do-nothing pro-life Republicans and want clear support for abolition over regulation.
questions.quote: “People are sick and tired of the do-nothing Republicans who have so departed from even their affirmed platform. And that’s why I won,” Jacob said. “I didn’t run any kind of smear campaign. I just made it clear that I stood for abolition and that my [Republican] opponent stood for the regulation of abortion.”

questions.question: What concrete actions does the article ask Indiana residents to take?
questions.answer: Sign the Abolish Abortion Indiana petition, call Judiciary Chair Rep. Jerry Torr to demand a hearing and vote, call seven specific representatives who promised to coauthor the bill but haven't, and attend the February 16 March for Abolition at the State Capitol.
questions.quote:
- Call Rep. Jerry Torr at (317) 234-9499 asking him to give a hearing and vote to the Protection at Conception Act.
- The following IN State Representatives publicly promised to coauthor the Protection at Conception Act while campaigning but have yet to do so. Call them to remind them of their promise to their voters.

questions.question: Why does Jacob argue the bill has no excuse to die in committee this year?
questions.answer: Because Republicans hold a supermajority and dominate the Judiciary Committee, Jacob says there is no procedural or political reason for the Protection at Conception Act not to receive a hearing and vote.
questions.quote: “The Protection at Conception Act is a good and righteous bill,” Jacob said. “It should be voted on, and with a Republican majority in that committee, there’s no reason it should die in committee.”

related.title: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
related.reason: Background on the prior session in which the same Indiana Protection at Conception Act was defeated, including Speaker Bosma's role referenced directly in this article.

related.title: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
related.reason: Covers Jacob's primary victory over Dollyne Sherman, the upset cited in this article as proof that abolition is winning Indiana voters.

related.title: John Jacob Wins!
related.reason: Reports Jacob's general election victory, the precondition for him being seated to coauthor HB1539; directly hyperlinked from this article.

related.title: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
related.reason: Parallel state-level abolition bill filed weeks after HB1539, useful for comparing legislative strategy across states in the same session.

related.title: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
related.reason: Another 2021 sister bill that, like HB1539, attempts immediate abolition rather than incremental regulation.

related.title: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
related.reason: Demonstrates the broader 2021 multi-state pattern of legislators filing equal-protection abolition bills, of which Indiana's is one example.

related.title: Pro-Life Oklahoman Who Voted to Table Bill to Abolish Abortion: Julie Daniels
related.reason: Illustrates the same dynamic Jacob describes in Indiana: self-identified pro-life Republicans blocking abolition bills inside committee.
